title: Today's Tech Brief: Hackers duped Meta AI support chatbot to steal celebrity Instagram accounts
summary: Hackers tricked Meta's AI support chatbot into giving them access to celebrity Instagram accounts.
imageAlt: Image from source article for Hackers duped Meta AI support chatbot to steal celebrity Instagram accounts
imageCaption: Hackers duped Meta AI support chatbot to steal celebrity Instagram accounts
imageSourceTitle: Hackers duped Meta AI support chatbot to steal celebrity Instagram accounts

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and I'm looking at some concerning news in the tech world today. It seems hackers have found a way to trick Meta's AI support chatbot into giving them access to celebrity Instagram accounts. This is a big deal, especially since it involves one of the biggest social media platforms out there. I'm talking about Instagram, where celebrities share their lives with millions of followers.

According to a report from Arstechnica, hackers were able to dupe Meta's AI support chatbot into providing them with access to notable Instagram accounts. This is interesting because it shows just how vulnerable even the most advanced AI systems can be to clever manipulation. It appears that the hackers were able to use a combination of social engineering and clever phrasing to get the chatbot to give up the access they needed. I'm curious, how do you outsmart an AI designed to help you?

In related news, a recent study found that many popular password managers have vulnerabilities that could expose user data. This is a concern for anyone who uses these services to generate and store unique passwords. It seems that even the tools designed to keep us safe aren't immune to flaws.

Hackers duped Meta AI support chatbot to steal celebrity Instagram accounts is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: Text settings Story text Size Small Standard Large Width * Standard Wide Links Standard Orange * Subscribers only Learn more Minimize to nav Meta’s AI support chatbot proved unusually helpful to hackers looking to steal and resell notable Instagram accounts—the hackers simply asking the bot to change the accounts’ associated email addresses while using VPN ... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
So that is the brief version: a few headlines, a little context, and just enough connective tissue to make the day easier to follow without turning the whole thing into homework.
